summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

If P(A) = 0.5 and P(A and B) = 0.2, what is P(B|A)?

P(B|A) = P(A and B) / P(A) = 0.2 / 0.5 = 0.4.

In a class, 60% play sports. Of those, 40% also play music. What is P(music | sports)?

P(music | sports) is given directly as 40% (0.40) — the percentage of sport-players who also play music.

A two-way table shows 20 students like math, 15 like science, and 8 like both. What is P(science | math)?

P(science | math) = P(both) / P(math) = 8/20 = 2/5.

In a survey, P(owns a dog) = 0.4 and P(owns a cat AND a dog) = 0.15. What is P(cat | dog)?

P(cat | dog) = P(cat and dog) / P(dog) = 0.15 / 0.4 = 0.375.

From a deck, a card is drawn and it is a face card. What is P(king | face card)?

There are 12 face cards (J, Q, K × 4 suits), and 4 are kings. P(king | face card) = 4/12 = 1/3.

A bag has 4 red and 6 blue. You draw one red. What is P(red on second draw | first was red, no replacement)?

After removing 1 red: 3 red remain out of 9 total. P = 3/9 = 1/3.

What is 'conditional probability'?

Conditional probability focuses on a reduced sample space — only the outcomes where the given condition is true.

What is 'joint probability'?

Joint probability P(A and B) is the probability that both events A and B occur together.

What is a 'two-way table' used for in conditional probability?

A two-way table cross-classifies data by two categorical variables, showing counts in each combination. Conditional probabilities are found by restricting to one row or column.

What is 'Bayes' Theorem' at a basic level?

Bayes' Theorem lets you 'flip' a conditional probability. If you know P(B|A), you can find P(A|B) using the formula.

What is the 'general multiplication rule'?

The general multiplication rule P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A) applies universally. For independent events, P(B|A) simplifies to P(B).

In a school, 70% of students pass math. Of those who pass math, 80% also pass science. What is P(pass both)?

P(both) = P(math) × P(science | math) = 0.70 × 0.80 = 0.56.

A doctor knows 5% of patients have a disease. A test detects it 90% of the time. If P(disease) = 0.05 and P(positive | disease) = 0.9, what is P(positive AND disease)?

P(positive and disease) = P(disease) × P(positive | disease) = 0.05 × 0.9 = 0.045.

A survey shows 200 people: 120 like coffee, 80 like tea, 50 like both. What is P(tea | coffee)?

P(tea | coffee) = 50/120 = 5/12. Among coffee drinkers, 50 out of 120 also like tea.

In a parking lot, 40% of cars are SUVs. Of the SUVs, 25% are black. What is the probability a random car is a black SUV?

P(black SUV) = P(SUV) × P(black | SUV) = 0.40 × 0.25 = 0.10 = 10%.

Out of 100 students, 60 study algebra, 45 study geometry, and 30 study both. Given a student studies algebra, what is the probability they also study geometry?

P(geometry | algebra) = 30/60 = 1/2.

A factory produces 1000 items daily. 50 are defective. Quality control catches 90% of defective items. What is P(caught | defective)?

P(caught | defective) is given directly as 90% (0.90) — quality control catches 90% of defective items.

A company finds that 30% of employees exercise regularly. Among exercisers, 10% call in sick monthly. Among non-exercisers, 25% call in sick. What is P(sick | exerciser)?

P(sick | exerciser) is given directly as 10% (0.10).

At a pet store, 50 animals are for sale: 20 dogs, 15 cats, 15 fish. Of the dogs, 8 are puppies. What is P(puppy | dog)?

P(puppy | dog) = 8/20 = 2/5. Among the 20 dogs, 8 are puppies.

A store finds that 40% of shoppers buy groceries and 20% buy both groceries and household items. What is P(household | groceries)?

P(household | groceries) = P(both) / P(groceries) = 0.20 / 0.40 = 0.50.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What is the 'general multiplication rule'?

The general multiplication rule P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A) applies universally. For independent events, P(B|A) simplifies to P(B).
